title: Electronic Final States in Nuclear $β$ Decay: A Sudden-Approximation Framework
abstract: Electronic final states generated by sudden changes of the Hamiltonian are studied here, with emphasis on nuclear charge variation in $β$ decay. A $λ$-parametrized family $\hat H(λ)$ that continuously connects the initial and final Hamiltonians, so that the electronic response can be represented as a continuous deformation in Hilbert space, is introduced. Within the sudden approximation, transition amplitudes are written as overlaps between eigenstates of distinct Hamiltonians. To relate non-orthogonal one-electron basis sets in a stable way, the paper uses a practical transport scheme based on overlap metrics and truncated singular value decomposition (SVD). This mapping is interpreted as a discrete counterpart of continuous transport along the $λ$ path. The formalism is first developed for the one-electron case, where analytic structure and selection rules are made explicit, and then generalized to many-electron systems via nonorthogonal determinant overlap expressions. The resulting formulation gives transition probabilities in bound and continuum channels in a way that is both numerically stable and easy to interpret.
summary: This paper presents a new theoretical framework for modeling how electrons respond to the sudden change in nuclear charge during beta decay. By introducing a continuous interpolation parameter (lambda), the author creates a 'bridge' between the initial and final electronic states, solving the numerical instability caused by non-orthogonal basis sets. The method is successfully applied to a one-electron tritium decay model and generalized to complex many-electron systems. This approach provides a stable way to calculate both bound-state populations and ionization probabilities.
main_problem: Developing a mathematically consistent framework to calculate electronic final states (both bound and continuum) following the sudden change in nuclear charge during beta decay.
main_result: The paper establishes a robust many-electron framework using a lambda-homotopy to relate non-orthogonal initial and final states, validated by an analytical one-electron benchmark for tritium decay.
method: A sudden-approximation framework using a lambda-parametrized Hamiltonian family, a transport scheme based on SVD and overlap metrics, and multireference wave-function methods (CAS/RAS).
model_or_system: The system is nuclear beta decay (e.g., tritium decay) where a change in nuclear charge (Z) occurs. The model uses a lambda-homotopy Hamiltonian H(lambda) to continuously connect the initial and final electronic Hamiltonians.
key_observables: Transition amplitudes (c_n), transition probabilities for bound and continuum channels, and a completeness diagnostic (delta_comp).
important_parameters: Nuclear charge (Z), interpolation parameter (lambda), reduced mass (mu_N), and the sudden approximation limit.
main_assumptions: The sudden approximation (nuclear decay is much faster than electronic relaxation) and a non-relativistic treatment of the electronic motion.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of the sudden approximation process; Figure 2 illustrates how increasing nuclear charge contracts electronic density and induces level splitting/mixing.
paper_structure: The paper begins with the physical premise of the sudden approximation, develops a one-electron analytical benchmark using a lambda-path, generalizes the formalism to many-electron systems using determinant overlaps, and concludes with a structured 6-step computational methodology.
why_interesting: The use of a lambda-homotopy to regularize the transition between non-orthogonal Hilbert spaces and the development of a transport scheme for tracking eigensubspaces is highly relevant to many-body dynamics and the study of non-adiabatic transitions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
